test_name: Verify exact column count matches ICD
description: Validate that the employees table in BigQuery contains exactly 14 columns as specified in the ICD.
precondition: The employees table must exist in the target BigQuery dataset and be accessible.
steps: 1. Execute query against INFORMATION_SCHEMA.COLUMNS for employees table  2. Count total number of columns returned  3. Compare count to ICD-specified value of 14  4. Verify expected count matches actual
query: SELECT COUNT(*) AS column_count FROM <project>.<dataset>.INFORMATION_SCHEMA.COLUMNS WHERE table_name = 'employees'
sql_hint: SELECT COUNT(*) AS column_count FROM INFORMATION_SCHEMA.COLUMNS WHERE table_name = 'employees'

test_name: Verify column names and data types
description: Validate that all 14 column names and their data types in the employees table exactly match the ICD specification.
precondition: The employees table must exist in the target BigQuery dataset and all 14 columns must have been created by the migration pipeline.
input_data: Expected columns: EMPLOYEE_ID STRING, FIRST_NAME STRING, LAST_NAME STRING, EMAIL STRING, PHONE_NUMBER STRING, HIRE_DATE STRING, JOB_ID STRING, SALARY STRING, COMMISSION_PCT STRING, MANAGER_ID STRING, DEPARTMENT_ID STRING, STATUS STRING, CREATED_DATE STRING, UPDATED_DATE STRING
steps: 1. Execute query against INFORMATION_SCHEMA.COLUMNS for employees table  2. Retrieve column_name and data_type for all columns  3. Compare each column name and data type against ICD specification  4. Flag any mismatches in name or data type  5. Verify all 14 columns match exactly
query: SELECT column_name, data_type FROM <project>.<dataset>.INFORMATION_SCHEMA.COLUMNS WHERE table_name = 'employees' ORDER BY ordinal_position
sql_hint: SELECT column_name, data_type FROM INFORMATION_SCHEMA.COLUMNS WHERE table_name = 'employees' ORDER BY ordinal_position
expected_result: All 14 rows returned with column_name and data_type matching ICD; zero mismatches

test_name: Null Check on Primary Key Column
description: Validate that the EMPLOYEE_ID column contains no NULL values in the employees table, as it is defined as the primary key.
precondition: The employees table must be fully loaded in BigQuery under <project>.<dataset> with at least one batch of data present.
steps: 1. Execute the query to count NULL values in EMPLOYEE_ID  2. Capture the returned count  3. Verify the count equals 0
query: SELECT COUNT(*) AS null_count FROM <project>.<dataset>.employees WHERE EMPLOYEE_ID IS NULL
sql_hint: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM employees WHERE EMPLOYEE_ID IS NULL
expected_result: null_count = 0; no NULL values exist in EMPLOYEE_ID

test_name: Empty String Check on EMAIL Column
description: Validate that the EMAIL column contains no empty string or whitespace-only values in the employees table.
precondition: The employees table must be fully loaded in BigQuery under <project>.<dataset> with representative data including potential empty string entries.
steps: 1. Execute the query to count rows where EMAIL is an empty string or whitespace-only  2. Capture the returned count  3. Verify the count equals 0
query: SELECT COUNT(*) AS empty_count FROM <project>.<dataset>.employees WHERE TRIM(EMAIL) = '' OR EMAIL = ''
sql_hint: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM employees WHERE TRIM(EMAIL) = '' OR EMAIL = ''
expected_result: empty_count = 0; no empty or whitespace-only values in EMAIL

description: Validate that an EMAIL value of exactly 100 characters is accepted and stored without truncation in BigQuery.
precondition: A row exists in <project>.<dataset>.employees where EMAIL is exactly 100 characters long.
steps: 1. Insert a row with EMAIL value of exactly 100 characters into the source system. 2. Run the pipeline to load data into <project>.<dataset>.employees. 3. Query BigQuery for the inserted EMPLOYEE_ID. 4. Check LENGTH(EMAIL) = 100. 5. Verify no truncation occurred.
query: SELECT EMPLOYEE_ID, EMAIL, LENGTH(EMAIL) AS email_length FROM <project>.<dataset>.employees WHERE LENGTH(EMAIL) = 100 LIMIT 5;
sql_hint: SELECT LENGTH(EMAIL) FROM employees WHERE EMAIL = '<100-char-value>'
expected_result: LENGTH(EMAIL) equals 100; full value stored without truncation.

description: Validate that an EMAIL value of 101 characters is rejected or flagged as a data quality violation during pipeline ingestion.
precondition: A row exists in the source system where EMAIL is exactly 101 characters long, exceeding the varchar(100) source constraint.
steps: 1. Insert a row with EMAIL value of exactly 101 characters into the source system. 2. Run the pipeline to load data into <project>.<dataset>.employees. 3. Query BigQuery for the inserted EMPLOYEE_ID. 4. Check LENGTH(EMAIL) against 101. 5. Verify pipeline raises an error, rejects the row, or the value is not silently truncated.
query: SELECT EMPLOYEE_ID, EMAIL, LENGTH(EMAIL) AS email_length FROM <project>.<dataset>.employees WHERE LENGTH(EMAIL) > 100;
sql_hint: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM employees WHERE LENGTH(EMAIL) > 100
expected_result: Row count is 0; no EMAIL values exceeding 100 characters are present in target.
